text: The video shows the conventional positioning of the surgical port used for radical prostatectomy.
The surgeon begins the procedure by locating the vessels of the bladder, preparing for further dissection.
The surgeon opens the peritoneum, revealing the anatomical structures on the right side.
The surgeon dissects the vessels near the bladder, avoiding immediate approach to the ureter, with careful manipulation of surrounding tissues.
The surgeon identifies the umbilical artery and applies a clip to occlude it, visible within the surgical field.
The surgeon carefully dissects around the pelvic region, aiming to preserve the nerve that supplies the penis and pelvic muscles.
The surgeon dissects the bladder laterally, progressing towards the prostate, and attempts to isolate the benders at the base of the prostate, similar to the technique used in radical prostatectomy.
The surgeon pushes the prostate into view, avoiding the use of thermic coagulation during the procedure.
The surgeon dissects the visceral part of the endopelvic fascia, exposing the vesicle prostatic vessels at the level of the bladder neck. The vessels are carefully cut at the base of the prostate.
The surgeon visually identifies the prostate, the neurovascular bundles, and the anatomical boundary between the bladder and prostate.
The surgeon completes the dissection of the right neurovascular bundle, visibly freeing it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon moves to the ureter after cutting the bladder vessel, with the ureter visibly located behind the umbilical artery.
The surgeon clips the urethra while carefully preserving the surrounding vascular structures, visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon opens the Douglas pouch, revealing the dissected vessels of the prostate.

The surgeon cuts the superior vesicular artery, controlling the residual vessel. The bladder and prostate are then moved completely to the left side, with the Deneuve-Villiers fascia being pushed aside.
The surgeon moves to dissect the left side of the bladder after completing the dissection on the right side, with the bladder showing carcinoma in situ on its superior part.
The surgeon isolates the umbilical artery and the superior vesicle artery, using clips and cold cutting techniques.
The surgeon removes the surrounding fat tissue from the bladder, performing an anatomical dissection to expose the bladder structure.
The surgeon opens the Douglas pouch, revealing the vas deferens and the left ureter. The left ureter is controlled to prevent any movement or damage during the procedure.
The surgeon performs a limited dissection of the ureter, carefully cutting to preserve the surrounding vascularization.
A small surgical clip is applied near the origin of the ureter. The bladder is visible, having been dissected posteriorly, with one or two small branches observed on the left side.
The surgeon identifies a vein originating from the bladder and prostate, extending towards the dorsal vein complex, and applies a clip measuring ten or five millimeters to secure it.
The bladder is shown completely dissected posteriorly. The surgeon proceeds to cut the left vas deferens.

The surgeon exposes the endopelvic fascia on the left side of the prostate, identifying distinct bundles posteriorly. The dissection begins on the right side, progresses posteriorly, and then moves to the left.
The surgeon exposes the posterior aspect of the prostate, highlighting the de non-villier fascia, which is preserved during the dissection.
The surgeon begins anterior dissection of the bladder after completing the posterior dissection, ensuring a clear operating field with the bladder and prostate fully separated.

The surgeon identifies the prostate, bladder, and dorsal vein complex, indicating the need to cut the dorsal vein complex while preserving the anterior fixation of the urethra. Muscles from the bladder neck to the pubis bone, referred to as aprons, are visible.
The surgeon carefully pushes the fascia close to the apex of the prostate, aiming to preserve surrounding structures for optimal early continence.

The surgeon visually identifies the bladder and the dorsal vein complex, noting that stitches are not required for control. The apex of the prostate is clearly visible, and dissection is performed laterally and posteriorly before proceeding anteriorly.
The laparoscopic view shows the dorsal vein complex with no visible bleeding, as the pressure from the insufflated gas at twelve millimeters of mercury effectively controls hemostasis.
The surgeon pushes the endopelvic fascia laterally to expose the limit of the prostate, reaching the apex. The catheter is visibly removed from the surgical field.
The surgeon exposes the urethra, focusing on preserving a good length of the structure. Minor bleeding is observed during the procedure.
The surgeon cuts the urethra at the level of the verru montanum, with the assistant placing a name block at the apex of the prostate. The prepared urethra is visible during the procedure.

The surgeon identifies the left ureter and main vessels, including the umbilical artery, during the cystectomy. The left ureter is visible behind the umbilical artery. Efforts are made to preserve the uterine vessels while maintaining visibility of the left ureter.

The surgeon examines the right ureter, noting the placement of a double G stent due to a tumor previously located at the left ureteral orifice. The surgical field shows multiple resections with a negative pathologic result.

The surgeon identifies the umbilical artery and ureter as key anatomical landmarks, performing lateral dissection of the bladder. The umbilical artery and superior vesicle artery are visible on the right side. Cold dissection is used to preserve vascularization and achieve a good functional result.
The surgeon identifies the round ligament and begins dissection between the bladder and uterus on the left side, with the iliac artery visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon clips the umbilical artery and the superior vesicle artery to facilitate a clear dissection of the bladder, ensuring the vessels are clearly identified.

The surgeon continues the dissection between the bladder and uterus, advancing the dissection towards the trigone area. An instrument is inserted into the vagina to aid in manipulation during the procedure.

An assistant holds a metallic valve in the vagina to provide a clear view. The surgeon performs a medial dissection under the bladder, carefully preserving the nerve structures and the lateral wall of the vagina.

On the right side, the surgeon encounters difficulty in dissection due to the patient's extensive history of bladder tumor resections. The inferior bladder artery is visible as the dissection proceeds under the bladder neck.

The surgeon carefully preserves the first part of the urethra, along with its vascularization and innervation, by proceeding millimeter by millimeter using clips. No thermal regulation is applied laterally. Upon reaching the balloon, the surgeon dissects the lateral face of the bladder, similar to the technique used in male patients.

The surgeon exposes the anterior aspect of the surgical field, identifying the vessels under the bladder neck. Lateral vessels are clipped, and the vagina is visible under the balloon. The dorsal vein complex is noted, similar to male anatomy. The surgeon cuts at the junction between the bladder and urethra, with the balloon clearly visible.

The surgeon inflates the balloon to five cc to facilitate a cold dissection around the bladder neck and the first part of the urethra. A clip is placed at the bladder neck, and the urethra is cut at this level.

The surgeon protects the nerves and muscles around the urethra. A left node dissection is performed, followed by opening at the anterior wall of the vagina. The bladder and nodes are removed, and the vagina is closed distally to prevent fistulas. The urethra is visible, and the neobladder reconstruction begins.

The surgeon identifies the right ureter and positions it behind the artery following the left node dissection. The right colon is visible, and the surgeon measures approximately 25 to 30 centimeters from the second loop. A small incision of about one centimeter is made to find a suitable loop leading to the urethra.

The surgeon sutures the ileal loop to the urethra using a rolling suture technique. The ileal loop is cut to a length of twenty-five to thirty centimeters on both the left and right sides. A lateral-lateral anastomosis is performed.
The surgeon opens the neobladder on the anti-mesenteric area of the bowel, creating a V-shaped incision.

The surgeon uses a ProGrasp instrument to close the posterior part of the neobladder with a V-Loc 3-0 suture, applying tension every two passages. The suture technique involves taking the musculosa layer for ease. The extremity of the posterior part is visible, and the surgeon proceeds with an anterior reconstruction of the urethra.
The surgeon performs an anterior reconstruction of the urethra over a two-centimeter segment. The neobladder is being shaped with two branches, as visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon uses a Prograsp instrument to hold the left part of the bladder, which visually resembles a heart shape. A 26-gauge needle is used to close the left part of the bladder, with the superior part of the posterior layer coming anteriorly.

The surgeon manipulates the right part of the bladder, performing multiple Studer maneuvers. The ureters are positioned in front of the neobladder without crossing the urethra, minimizing dissection. The morphology of the bladder is visible, and the surgeon tests the water tightness of the bladder.

The surgeon removes the ureter from behind the iliac artery and cuts it. The neobladder is opened, and a suture is performed using a 5-0 monocryl with a triangular needle, ensuring there is no tension. The posterior and anterior layers are visible, and the ureter is positioned without any tension, with no stents used.

The surgeon demonstrates the posterior layer of the neobladder and the ureteric implantation, performing between eight and ten passages to test the quality of the stitches. The bladder is opened, showcasing the codified laparoscopic cystectomy technique.

The surgeon uses a triangular needle to minimize tension and decrease traction while suturing, employing a two needle driver for precision. The grasper is used to manipulate the tissue during the procedure.

The surgeon uses a double G stent, typically eight French, to ensure the ureteric anastomosis is watertight, preserving the vascularization during fine dissection of the ureter. The final position of the neobladder is shown, with no leakage observed at the ureteric implantation site.
